seoTitle: How to Run Scrum and Kanban in the Same Jira Project
metaDescription: Learn how to combine Scrum and Kanban boards on the same Jira project so different teams can work their preferred way.
intro: Some organizations have teams that prefer sprint-based planning while others favor continuous flow, and Jira allows both approaches to coexist on the same underlying project. This guide covers setting up Scrum and Kanban boards together.

steps.heading: Confirm your project setup
steps.body: Make sure your project's issues are structured in a way that works for both sprint-based and continuous-flow tracking.

steps.heading: Create a Scrum board
steps.body: Add a Scrum board filtered to the project or a relevant subset of issues for teams that plan in sprints.

steps.heading: Create a Kanban board
steps.body: Add a separate Kanban board, filtered similarly, for teams or workstreams that prefer continuous flow without sprints.

steps.heading: Adjust filters to avoid overlap issues
steps.body: Fine-tune each board's filter so the two views show the right slice of work without confusing the whole team.

steps.heading: Align on shared statuses
steps.body: Keep the underlying workflow consistent so issues move sensibly regardless of which board a team is viewing them from.

faq.question: Can two boards really share the same project data?
faq.answer: Yes, boards are just filtered views on top of issue data, so multiple boards can pull from the same project or filter.

faq.question: Will sprints on the Scrum board affect the Kanban board?
faq.answer: Sprint assignment can influence what appears depending on each board's filter, so it's worth checking how issues flow between the two.

faq.question: Is this setup common for larger organizations?
faq.answer: Yes, it's a common pattern when different teams within one organization have different planning preferences.

faq.question: Do I need separate permission schemes for each board?
faq.answer: Boards themselves don't require separate permission schemes, since permissions are typically managed at the project level.
